root_text.sn17.3:1.3: Bhūtapubbaṁ, bhikkhave, aññatarasmiṁ udakarahade mahākummakulaṁ ciranivāsi ahosi.
root_text.sn17.3:1.4: Atha kho, bhikkhave, aññataro kummo aññataraṁ kummaṁ etadavoca:
root_text.sn17.3:1.5: ‘mā kho tvaṁ, tāta kumma, etaṁ padesaṁ agamāsī’ti.
root_text.sn17.3:1.6: Agamāsi kho, bhikkhave, so kummo taṁ padesaṁ.
root_text.sn17.3:1.8: Atha kho, bhikkhave, so kummo yena so kummo tenupasaṅkami.
root_text.sn17.3:1.9: Addasā kho, bhikkhave, so kummo taṁ kummaṁ dūratova āgacchantaṁ.
root_text.sn17.3:1.11: ‘kacci tvaṁ, tāta kumma, na taṁ padesaṁ agamāsī’ti?
root_text.sn17.3:1.14: ‘Akkhato khomhi, tāta kumma, anupahato, atthi ca me idaṁ suttakaṁ piṭṭhito piṭṭhito anubandhan’ti.
root_text.sn17.3:1.16: Etena hi te, tāta kumma, suttakena pitaro ca pitāmahā ca anayaṁ āpannā byasanaṁ āpannā.
root_text.sn17.3:1.17: Gaccha dāni tvaṁ, tāta kumma, na dāni tvaṁ amhākan’ti.
root_text.sn17.3:2.1: Luddoti kho, bhikkhave, mārassetaṁ pāpimato adhivacanaṁ.
root_text.sn17.3:2.4: Yo hi koci, bhikkhave, bhikkhu uppannaṁ lābhasakkārasilokaṁ assādeti nikāmeti—
root_text.sn17.3:2.5: ayaṁ vuccati, bhikkhave, bhikkhu giddho papatāya anayaṁ āpanno byasanaṁ āpanno yathākāmakaraṇīyo pāpimato.

translation_text.sn17.3:1.3: Once upon a time in a certain lake there was a large family of turtles that had lived there for a long time.
translation_text.sn17.3:1.4: Then one of the turtles said to another,
translation_text.sn17.3:1.5: ‘My dear turtle, don’t you go to that place.’
translation_text.sn17.3:1.6: But that turtle did go to that place,
translation_text.sn17.3:1.7: and a hunter pierced her with a harpoon.
translation_text.sn17.3:1.8: Then that turtle went back to the other turtle.
translation_text.sn17.3:1.9: When the other turtle saw her coming off in the distance,
translation_text.sn17.3:1.11: ‘My dear turtle, I hope you didn’t go to that place!’
translation_text.sn17.3:1.13: ‘But my dear turtle, I hope you’re not hurt or injured!’
translation_text.sn17.3:1.14: ‘I’m not hurt or injured. But this cord keeps dragging behind me.’
translation_text.sn17.3:1.15: ‘Indeed, my dear turtle, you’re hurt and injured!
translation_text.sn17.3:1.16: Your father and grandfather met with tragedy and disaster because of such a cord.
translation_text.sn17.3:1.17: Go now, you are no longer one of us.’
translation_text.sn17.3:2.1: ‘Hunter’ is a term for Māra the Wicked.
translation_text.sn17.3:2.2: ‘Harpoon’ is a term for possessions, honor, and popularity.
translation_text.sn17.3:2.3: ‘Cord’ is a term for greed and relishing.
translation_text.sn17.3:2.4: Whoever enjoys and likes arisen possessions, honor, and popularity is called a mendicant who has been pierced with a harpoon. They’ve met with tragedy and disaster, and the Wicked One can do with them what he wants.
translation_text.sn17.3:2.6: So grim are possessions, honor, and popularity. …”

variant_text.sn17.3:2.5: bhikkhu giddho papatāya → bhikkhu papatāya (sya-all, km); bhikkhu viddho papatāya (?)
